news_summary: Today's human rights news highlights progress in supporting refugees' economic contributions, advancing fair social benefits for mobile workers, honoring champions of humanity, and mobilizing global leaders to address housing insecurity.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité des droits de l'homme d'aujourd'hui met en avant les progrès réalisés pour soutenir la contribution économique des réfugiés, promouvoir des prestations sociales équitables pour les travailleurs mobiles, rendre hommage aux défenseurs de l'humanité et mobiliser les dirigeants mondiaux afin de lutter contre la précarité du logement.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy sobre derechos humanos destacan los avances en el apoyo a la contribución económica de los refugiados, la promoción de prestaciones sociales justas para los trabajadores migrantes, el reconocimiento a los defensores de la humanidad y la movilización de los líderes mundiales para hacer frente a la inseguridad en materia de vivienda.

articles.title: The invisible migration: How urban refugees are powering Uganda’s economy
articles.summary: As Paul Kithima and the entrepreneurs in Makindye demonstrate, when refugees are given the tools to succeed, they don’t just survive, they build the future of the country that welcomed them.
articles.content:
This article by Ayebare Cinderella was originally published by Peace News Network on April 8, 2026. An edited version is being republished on Global Voices under a content partnership agreement.

Uganda has long been celebrated globally for its progressive refugee policy, one that avoids the restrictive encampments seen in other nations in favor of a model of integration and freedom of movement.

According to statistics from the Office of the Prime Minister, Uganda was host to 1,961,518 refugees and asylum seekers as of October 31, 2025. Of these, 91 percent live in refugee settlements. While rural settlements are the traditional destination, a massive, largely invisible migration is reshaping the nation’s capital, with nine percent residing in urban areas. Thousands are trading the dwindling rations of settlements for the grit and opportunity of the Kampala metropolitan area.

In the bustling suburbs of Makindye and Zana, refugees from the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), Somalia, South Sudan, Eritrea, Sudan, Ethiopia, Rwanda, and Burundi are no longer just asylum seekers. They are taxpayers, employers, and entrepreneurs.

Refugees from these countries have fled harrowing violence and instability involving civil wars, political unrest, and other major upheavals. Yet, they are now using entrepreneurship, including food, clothing, and cosmetic services, to power Uganda’s economy and build harmony amongst the diverse groups within refugee and host communities.

The stitch of resilience: John Babish Makando

In Makindye Division, a suburb characterized by its diverse refugee population, John Babish Makando operates a tailoring business that defies the common stereotypes of the trade. Having fled the conflict in Eastern DRC in 2019, Makando bypassed the settlement system entirely, betting on his skills to survive in the competitive urban landscape of Kampala.

For Makando, being a refugee is not a barrier to professional excellence. He has built a reputation for punctuality and precision, qualities he says are often lacking in the local market. Makando explains to Peace News Network (PNN):

I am removing the common client misperception about local tailors. Many are known for letting down clients by not meeting deadlines or spoiling designs. Here, it is different. I focus on quality and being a good timekeeper.

Makando’s contribution to the Ugandan economy is tangible. Unlike refugees in settlements who receive humanitarian aid, urban refugees like Makando receive nearly zero financial support. Instead, in Kampala, small-scale entrepreneurs are the backbone of the local economy, contributing through structured taxes. Most pay an annual Kampala City Council Authority (KCCA) Trading License, ranging from USD 8.34 (UGX 31,500) for basic stalls to USD 23.83 (UGX 90,000) for salons and workshops. Additionally, artisans pay a Local Service Tax based on income, while market vendors contribute through daily dues. Despite limited capital, these micro-businesses ensure steady revenue for urban development and city infrastructure. Makando pays rent for his tailoring shop and fulfills his tax obligations, fuelling local development.

His journey to self-reliance began with a three-month vocational training course sponsored by the International Rescue Committee (IRC) and Great Lakes Peace and Reconciliation Ministries (GRS). Today, he is paying that gift forward. Makando noted:

I went for a three-month skill training and after that I did a short course in tailoring. After some years of getting experience, we decided to start transferring these skills to fellow refugees and host communities by training them how to make clothes using different fabrics.

He added:

We decided to teach people to show them how we are connected to our Ugandan neighbours, because there is a saying that my neighbour is my first family.

Blending fashion and food: Amina’s fresh market

The economic ecosystem created by refugees extends from the wardrobe to the dinner table.

Amina, another Congolese refugee in Makindye, ensures both refugees and Ugandans eat well. Amina has tapped into the high demand for fresh produce in the densely populated corridors of Makindye.

Her stall is a vibrant intersection of cultures. She specializes in vegetables like tomatoes and greens, but her real success lies in sombe (cassava leaves), susha, dodo, and eggplants, staples that appeal to Rwandan, Burundian, and Sudanese clients. Amina notes:

Ugandans love Congolese food and Congolese love Ugandan food.

Amina represents a form of cultural adaptation that goes beyond mere survival. By maintaining her stall, Amina is not just providing for her family; She is an active participant in the city’s informal economy, contributing to the tax base and promoting urban food security.

Turning migrants into economic engines

At the heart of this transformation is the Bondeko Refugee Livelihoods Center in Najjanankumbi’s Stella Village. Founded in 1997 by Reverend Father Michael Lingisi, Bondeko has evolved from a simple safety net into a sophisticated engine of self-reliance.

Executive Director, Paul Kithima, is a vocal advocate for localization,arguing that the best solutions for refugees come from the refugees themselves. Kithima said:

We have livelihood activities where we train refugees in different areas like hairdressing, tailoring, ICT, and makeup.

He added that the center also has savings groups, which provide essential training in entrepreneurship and financial literacy.

We have a total of 37 saving groups, attending to 1,110 individuals, with each group comprising 30 people.

He continued:

We help them to register as Community Based Organizations (CBOs) so they can access financial institutions to explore options like loans and other products.

Last year alone, Bondeko assisted over 3,500 people, including both refugees and Ugandan nationals. This inclusive approach, training nationals alongside refugees, is key to maintaining social cohesion and preventing resentment.

The center’s programs are diverse, ranging from a modernized bakery and mushroom farms to a law clinic that helps refugee families navigate asylum status and a youth center for children.

A new chapter of self-reliance

The shift toward urban entrepreneurship has become a necessity. With 2026 funding cuts slashing settlement rations to near-zero, the invisible migration is no longer a choice but a survival strategy. These individuals are moving away from aid dependency and toward active economic participation.

As Paul Kithima and the entrepreneurs in Makindye demonstrate, when refugees are given the tools to succeed, they don’t just survive; they build the future of the country that welcomed them.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights Uganda's progressive refugee policy enabling urban refugees to become self-reliant entrepreneurs who contribute significantly to the local economy and social cohesion. It details tangible economic impacts, vocational training, and inclusive community programs benefiting thousands, demonstrating a broad, meaningful positive societal change.
articles.personality_title: Urban refugees in Uganda build businesses and boost local economy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Uganda hosts nearly two million refugees, mostly in rural settlements, but a growing number are moving to cities like Kampala. Unlike many countries, Uganda allows refugees to live freely outside camps, offering them a chance to work and start businesses.

**What happened** – Refugees from countries like the Democratic Republic of Congo, Somalia, and South Sudan are starting shops, tailoring businesses, and food stalls in Kampala’s neighborhoods such as Makindye. People like John Babish Makando, a tailor, and Amina, a fresh produce seller, are contributing to the local economy by paying taxes and creating jobs. They learned skills through training programs supported by organizations like the International Rescue Committee and the Bondeko Refugee Livelihoods Center.

**Impact** – These urban refugees are shifting from relying on aid to becoming self-reliant entrepreneurs. Their businesses not only provide income but also help improve city services through taxes. They bring diverse cultures together, promote social harmony, and support both refugee and Ugandan communities. This change helps refugees build a new life and strengthens the city’s economy.

**What's next step** – With aid in rural settlements decreasing, more refugees are expected to move to cities and start businesses. Training centers like Bondeko will continue to teach skills and financial literacy, helping refugees and locals work together. This could lead to more jobs, better social integration, and stronger local economies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Refugees in Uganda’s cities are turning challenges into opportunities by creating businesses that support themselves and the communities around them.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les réfugiés urbains en Ouganda créent des entreprises et dynamisent l’économie locale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Ouganda accueille près de deux millions de réfugiés, principalement dans des camps ruraux, mais un nombre croissant s’installe dans des villes comme Kampala. Contrairement à beaucoup d’autres pays, l’Ouganda permet aux réfugiés de vivre librement hors des camps, leur offrant la possibilité de travailler et de créer des entreprises.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des réfugiés venus de pays comme la République démocratique du Congo, la Somalie et le Soudan du Sud ouvrent des boutiques, des ateliers de couture et des étals de produits frais dans des quartiers de Kampala tels que Makindye. Des personnes comme John Babish Makando, tailleur, et Amina, vendeuse de légumes, contribuent à l’économie locale en payant des impôts et en créant des emplois. Ils ont appris leurs compétences grâce à des programmes de formation soutenus par des organisations comme le Comité international de secours et le Centre Bondeko.

**Impact** – Ces réfugiés urbains passent de la dépendance à l’aide à l’autonomie grâce à leurs entreprises. Leurs activités génèrent des revenus et améliorent les services de la ville via les taxes. Ils favorisent aussi la diversité culturelle, la cohésion sociale et soutiennent tant les réfugiés que les Ougandais. Ce changement aide les réfugiés à reconstruire leur vie et renforce l’économie locale.

**Prochaine étape** – Avec la réduction de l’aide dans les camps ruraux, davantage de réfugiés devraient rejoindre les villes et créer des entreprises. Les centres de formation comme Bondeko continueront à enseigner des compétences et la gestion financière, encourageant la collaboration entre réfugiés et locaux. Cela pourrait entraîner plus d’emplois, une meilleure intégration sociale et des économies locales plus fortes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les réfugiés des villes ougandaises transforment leurs défis en opportunités en créant des entreprises qui soutiennent leurs communautés et elles-mêmes.
articles.personality_title_es: Los refugiados urbanos en Uganda crean negocios y fortalecen la economía local
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Uganda alberga a casi dos millones de refugiados, en su mayoría en asentamientos rurales, pero un número creciente se está mudando a ciudades como Kampala. A diferencia de otros países, Uganda permite que los refugiados vivan libremente fuera de los campamentos, dándoles la oportunidad de trabajar y emprender.

**Qué pasó** – Refugiados de países como la República Democrática del Congo, Somalia y Sudán del Sur están abriendo tiendas, talleres de costura y puestos de alimentos frescos en barrios de Kampala como Makindye. Personas como John Babish Makando, un sastre, y Amina, una vendedora de verduras, contribuyen a la economía local pagando impuestos y generando empleo. Aprendieron sus habilidades a través de programas de capacitación apoyados por organizaciones como el Comité Internacional de Rescate y el Centro de Medios de Vida para Refugiados Bondeko.

**Impacto** – Estos refugiados urbanos están pasando de depender de la ayuda a ser empresarios autosuficientes. Sus negocios no solo generan ingresos, sino que también mejoran los servicios de la ciudad mediante los impuestos. Fomentan la diversidad cultural, la armonía social y apoyan tanto a las comunidades refugiadas como a las locales. Este cambio ayuda a los refugiados a construir una nueva vida y fortalece la economía local.

**Próximo paso** – Con la reducción de la ayuda en los asentamientos rurales, se espera que más refugiados se muden a las ciudades y emprendan negocios. Centros de capacitación como Bondeko continuarán enseñando habilidades y educación financiera, ayudando a refugiados y locales a trabajar juntos. Esto podría generar más empleos, mejor integración social y economías locales más fuertes.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los refugiados en las ciudades de Uganda están convirtiendo los desafíos en oportunidades creando negocios que apoyan a sus comunidades y a ellos mismos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a bustling urban market scene in Kampala depicted through colorful woven fabrics hanging like vibrant banners, neatly arranged tailoring tools beside a sewing machine silhouette, and a lively fresh produce stall overflowing with cassava leaves, tomatoes, and eggplants, all set against a backdrop of simple, earthy-toned buildings symbolizing community and economic growth.

articles.title: Press release - MEPs back fairer and clearer rules on social benefits for EU mobile workers
articles.summary: The updated rules seek to ensure access to social security for EU workers who live or work in another EU country, while distributing obligations fairly among member states.Committee on Employment and Social Affairs Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
Parliament’s Employment and Social Affairs Committee has endorsed the provisional agreement with EU governments on updated rules for the coordination of social security systems by 47 votes in favour and 3 against, with 4 abstentions.



If formally adopted by Parliament and Council, the deal will introduce clearer criteria for determining which country’s social security legislation applies. It should also foster cooperation between member states, so that the necessary information is shared promptly, to help identify errors or fraud, including abusive practices, such as letterbox companies.



Unemployment benefits



The agreement clarifies how periods of work, self-employment, or insurance cover completed in different member states are counted when assessing entitlement to unemployment benefits. The member state where a person last worked or was insured will be responsible if the person was active there for at least one uninterrupted month.



People who go to another EU country to look for work will be entitled to receive unemployment benefits for six months from the country they left. This period may be extended until the end of their entitlement.



Regarding cross-border workers, the updated law clarifies which member state is responsible for paying benefits. If an unemployed cross-border worker has been employed, self-employed, and/or insured for an uninterrupted period of 22 weeks in a member state, benefits will be paid by that member state.



Family benefits



The revised rules clarify the distinction between family benefits in cash, intended to replace income when a person gives up or reduces work to raise a child, and other family benefits. This will promote a more equal sharing of child-raising responsibilities and removes possible financial disincentives for parents who reduce their working time to care for their child.



Long-term care benefits



The agreement clarifies how long-term care benefits should be coordinated across the EU, increasing legal certainty for people who need care and those who care for them. It adds a new definition of long-term care benefits and a list of benefits covered by these rules.



Workers sent to work in another EU country



Workers or self-employed persons sent abroad for up to 24 months (not replacing a previously sent employee) remain insured in the EU country where their employer is established or where they normally pursue their self-employed activity. To tackle fraud and errors, they must have been insured for at least 3 months before being sent abroad.



The agreement introduces a mandatory prior notification system: when a worker carries out activities in another EU-country, the competent authorities of the sending member state have to be notified in advance, except when it concerns business trips and short-term activities lasting no more than 3 consecutive days within a period of 30 consecutive days. The construction sector will not be part of this exception.



Working in two or more member states



For workers carrying out activities in two or more member states, the updated rules provide additional guidance on determining the “registered office or place of business” of the employer or business. This will help establish which member state’s social security legislation applies. To determine the place of business, relevant factors include the place where essential decisions are taken, where the turnover is generated, and the places where the general meetings are held.



Economically inactive citizens



Finally, negotiators agreed, in line with decisions by EU Court of Justice, that economically inactive mobile citizens should have comprehensive sickness insurance coverage in their host member state.



Quote



Gabriele Bischoff (S&D, DE) rapporteur for the file, said: “Today’s committee vote is the next major step towards clearer, fairer and more enforceable rules for everyone who lives or works across borders in the EU. Mobile workers must be able to rely on their social security rights, whether they are looking for work, caring for a child, in need of long-term care, or sent to another member state by their employer. After almost 10 years of negotiations, this agreement shows that Europe can deliver: it protects workers, supports fair mobility, strengthens trust between national systems and strengthens coordination between member states to tackle abuse. All eyes are now on the plenary vote, so we can finally update these long-overdue rules and deliver more legal certainty for workers, companies and member states alike.”



Next steps



Both Parliament and the Council need to adopt the provisional agreement formally before the rules can enter into force.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the EU Parliament committee endorsing updated social security coordination rules for mobile EU workers, which will provide clearer, fairer access to social benefits such as unemployment, family, and long-term care benefits. This has broad, tangible positive impact on millions of workers across the EU by protecting their rights and ensuring fair treatment across borders. The article provides detailed context on the new rules and their significance, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with meaningful societal benefit.
articles.personality_title: EU Parliament committee supports clearer social security rules for mobile workers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people in the European Union (EU) live or work in a different EU country than where they come from. This can make it hard to know which country’s social benefits, like unemployment or family support, they can use.

**What happened** – The EU Parliament’s Employment and Social Affairs Committee voted to approve new rules that make it clearer which country’s social security laws apply to workers who move between EU countries. The vote happened recently, with most members supporting the update. These rules explain how unemployment benefits, family benefits, and long-term care support will work for people who move or work across borders.

**Impact** – These changes help workers understand their rights better and make sure they get the benefits they deserve, no matter which EU country they work in. For example, people looking for jobs in another country can get unemployment help from their home country for six months. The rules also make it easier to stop fraud by requiring advance notice when workers move for jobs. This fairness makes working in different countries simpler and safer.

**What's next step** – The new rules still need to be officially approved by the full Parliament and the EU Council. After that, they will start to protect millions of mobile workers and improve cooperation between countries on social benefits.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The EU is updating social security rules to make sure workers moving between member countries get fair and clear access to benefits like unemployment and family support.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le comité du Parlement européen soutient des règles plus claires sur la sécurité sociale pour les travailleurs mobiles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses personnes dans l’Union européenne (UE) vivent ou travaillent dans un pays de l’UE différent de leur pays d’origine. Cela rend parfois difficile de savoir à quels avantages sociaux, comme le chômage ou le soutien familial, elles ont droit.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le comité Emploi et Affaires sociales du Parlement européen a voté pour approuver de nouvelles règles qui clarifient quelles lois de sécurité sociale s’appliquent aux travailleurs qui se déplacent entre les pays de l’UE. Le vote a eu lieu récemment, avec un large soutien. Ces règles expliquent comment fonctionnent les allocations chômage, les prestations familiales et l’aide aux soins de longue durée pour les personnes qui travaillent ou vivent à l’étranger.

**Impact** – Ces changements aident les travailleurs à mieux comprendre leurs droits et à recevoir les prestations auxquelles ils ont droit, quel que soit le pays de l’UE où ils travaillent. Par exemple, les chercheurs d’emploi dans un autre pays peuvent recevoir des allocations chômage de leur pays d’origine pendant six mois. Les règles facilitent aussi la lutte contre la fraude en exigeant une notification préalable lorsque les travailleurs sont envoyés à l’étranger. Cette équité rend le travail dans différents pays plus simple et plus sûr.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces règles doivent encore être officiellement adoptées par le Parlement complet et le Conseil de l’UE. Après cela, elles protégeront des millions de travailleurs mobiles et amélioreront la coopération entre pays sur les prestations sociales.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – L’UE met à jour les règles de sécurité sociale pour garantir aux travailleurs mobiles un accès clair et équitable aux prestations comme le chômage et le soutien familial.
articles.personality_title_es: El comité del Parlamento Europeo apoya reglas más claras sobre seguridad social para trabajadores móviles
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas en la Unión Europea (UE) viven o trabajan en un país diferente al suyo dentro de la UE. Esto puede dificultar saber a qué beneficios sociales, como el desempleo o el apoyo familiar, tienen derecho.

**Qué pasó** – El comité de Empleo y Asuntos Sociales del Parlamento Europeo aprobó nuevas reglas que aclaran qué leyes de seguridad social se aplican a los trabajadores que se mueven entre países de la UE. La votación fue reciente y contó con amplio apoyo. Estas reglas explican cómo funcionarán los beneficios de desempleo, los beneficios familiares y la atención a largo plazo para quienes trabajan o viven en otro país.

**Impacto** – Estos cambios ayudan a los trabajadores a entender mejor sus derechos y a recibir los beneficios que merecen, sin importar en qué país de la UE trabajen. Por ejemplo, las personas que buscan empleo en otro país pueden recibir beneficios de desempleo de su país de origen durante seis meses. Las reglas también facilitan la lucha contra el fraude al exigir notificación previa cuando los trabajadores son enviados al extranjero. Esta justicia hace que trabajar en diferentes países sea más fácil y seguro.

**Próximo paso** – Las nuevas reglas deben ser aprobadas oficialmente por todo el Parlamento y el Consejo de la UE. Después, protegerán a millones de trabajadores móviles y mejorarán la cooperación entre países sobre beneficios sociales.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La UE actualiza las reglas de seguridad social para garantizar que los trabajadores móviles tengan acceso justo y claro a beneficios como el desempleo y el apoyo familiar.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a balanced scale made of interlocking puzzle pieces, each piece decorated with subtle EU flag motifs, symbolizing cooperation between member states; on one side, stylized silhouettes of diverse workers connected by flowing lines representing social benefits, and on the other side, interconnected buildings symbolizing governments sharing information, all rendered in warm natural tones with soft, harmonious lighting.

articles.title: UN mourns CNN founder Ted Turner, a ‘tireless champion for our common humanity’
articles.summary: The American businessman, media mogul and philanthropist Ted Turner is being remembered by the United Nations for his long-standing support for the Organization and its values.
articles.content:
The founder of the pioneering Cable News Network, CNN – which created the global 24-hour news cycle – died on Wednesday at his home in Florida. He was 87.

UN Secretary-General António Guterres was deeply saddened to learn of his passing. He described Mr. Turner as “a visionary whose conviction, generosity and audacious spirit left a lasting imprint on the United Nations and our world.”

Tweet URL

Billion-dollar UN boost

“Ted Turner believed in the United Nations – and in its purpose – at a defining moment. And he invested in that mission,” the Secretary-General said in a statement.

“Ted Turner bet on humanity – and in the process helped strengthen the security of his own country and reinforce our common values.”

In September 1997, Mr. Turner pledged $1 billion to the UN to support the Organization and its causes.

This commitment – and the establishment of the United Nations Foundation the following year – represented the largest individual philanthropic gift in history at the time.

The Foundation – which operates as an independent charity – works closely with the UN on core priorities such as sustainable development, preventing disease, and promoting gender equality.

A ‘bridgebuilder’ and champion

Elizabeth Cousens, the President and CEO, said Mr. Turner “was a consummate bridgebuilder and tireless champion for our common humanity as well as our shared responsibility to steward our exquisite and fragile planet for the future.”

Following a devastating earthquake in Pakistan some two decades ago, the UN Foundation committed $1 million to support the immediate response efforts, in addition to establishing a fund to help other affected countries.

“Once again, survivors of disaster have turned to the UN and the international community in their time of need,” Mr. Turner said at the time.

“The world needs the UN’s leadership in these times, and the UN needs the world's support.”

UN Photo/Evan Schneider

A lasting legacy

The Secretary-General said Mr. Turner’s legacy goes beyond his commitment of resources, underscoring his ongoing advocacy for the United Nations and the importance of diplomacy.

“Ted Turner never stopped pushing for the world to do better, and to do better through the United Nations,” he recalled.

“He helped expand our reach, mobilise partnerships, and drive action on many of the defining challenges of our time – from global health to peace, human rights, and the protection of our planet.”

The Secretary-General concluded the statement by expressing deep gratitude “for his extraordinary commitment to international problem-solving and his unwavering support for the ideals of the United Nations Charter.”

He said Mr. Turner’s legacy endures “as we keep striving for a stronger United Nations and a better world for all.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the philanthropic legacy of Ted Turner, whose unprecedented $1 billion pledge to the United Nations created the UN Foundation that supports broad global causes including sustainable development, disease prevention, and gender equality. His contributions have had a significant and lasting positive impact on international humanitarian efforts and global cooperation, benefiting many people worldwide. The story focuses on his meaningful legacy and ongoing advocacy for global betterment through the UN, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with broad significance and substance.
articles.personality_title: Ted Turner’s $1 Billion Gift Strengthened the United Nations’ Global Work
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Ted Turner was an American businessman who created CNN, the first 24-hour news channel. Beyond media, he was a generous supporter of the United Nations (UN), an international group that works for peace, health, and equality around the world.

**What happened** – Ted Turner passed away at age 87. He is remembered for pledging $1 billion to the UN in 1997, the largest personal donation at that time. This money helped start the United Nations Foundation, a group that supports important causes like fighting disease, protecting the environment, and promoting equal rights for women.

**Impact** – Turner’s donation and ongoing support helped the UN respond to disasters, like earthquakes, and tackle big problems through partnerships and new projects. His work made it easier for the UN to help more people and showed how one person’s generosity can support global teamwork.

**What’s next step** – The United Nations Foundation continues its work using Turner’s gift to back UN goals. Leaders say his example inspires others to support the UN’s efforts for a safer and healthier world.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Ted Turner’s historic $1 billion gift created a foundation that keeps helping the United Nations solve global challenges and save lives.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le don d’un milliard de dollars de Ted Turner a renforcé le travail mondial des Nations Unies
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Ted Turner était un homme d’affaires américain qui a créé CNN, la première chaîne d’information 24h/24. Au-delà des médias, il a été un généreux soutien des Nations Unies (ONU), un groupe international qui travaille pour la paix, la santé et l’égalité dans le monde.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Ted Turner est décédé à l’âge de 87 ans. Il est reconnu pour avoir fait en 1997 un don d’un milliard de dollars à l’ONU, le plus important don personnel à cette époque. Cet argent a permis de créer la Fondation des Nations Unies, qui soutient des causes importantes comme la lutte contre les maladies, la protection de l’environnement et la promotion des droits des femmes.

**Impact** – Le don de Turner et son soutien continu ont aidé l’ONU à répondre aux catastrophes, comme les tremblements de terre, et à relever de grands défis grâce à des partenariats et de nouveaux projets. Son travail a facilité l’aide de l’ONU à un plus grand nombre de personnes et a montré comment la générosité d’une personne peut soutenir la coopération mondiale.

**Prochaine étape** – La Fondation des Nations Unies poursuit son travail en utilisant le don de Turner pour soutenir les objectifs de l’ONU. Les dirigeants disent que son exemple inspire d’autres personnes à soutenir les efforts de l’ONU pour un monde plus sûr et en meilleure santé.

**À retenir en une phrase** – Le don historique d’un milliard de dollars de Ted Turner a créé une fondation qui continue d’aider les Nations Unies à relever les défis mondiaux et à sauver des vies.
articles.personality_title_es: El don de 1.000 millones de dólares de Ted Turner fortaleció el trabajo global de la ONU
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Ted Turner fue un empresario estadounidense que creó CNN, el primer canal de noticias las 24 horas. Más allá de los medios, apoyó generosamente a las Naciones Unidas (ONU), un grupo internacional que trabaja por la paz, la salud y la igualdad en el mundo.

**Qué pasó** – Ted Turner falleció a los 87 años. Es recordado por prometer 1.000 millones de dólares a la ONU en 1997, la mayor donación personal en ese momento. Ese dinero ayudó a crear la Fundación de las Naciones Unidas, que apoya causas importantes como combatir enfermedades, proteger el medio ambiente y promover la igualdad de género.

**Impacto** – La donación y el apoyo continuo de Turner ayudaron a la ONU a responder a desastres, como terremotos, y a enfrentar grandes problemas mediante alianzas y nuevos proyectos. Su trabajo facilitó que la ONU ayudara a más personas y mostró cómo la generosidad de una persona puede apoyar el trabajo conjunto global.

**Próximo paso** – La Fundación de las Naciones Unidas sigue trabajando con el dinero de Turner para apoyar las metas de la ONU. Los líderes dicen que su ejemplo inspira a otros a apoyar los esfuerzos de la ONU para un mundo más seguro y saludable.

**Resumen en una frase** – El don histórico de 1.000 millones de dólares de Ted Turner creó una fundación que continúa ayudando a la ONU a resolver desafíos mundiales y salvar vidas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a radiant globe embraced by interconnected hands forming a protective circle, with a luminous ribbon of flowing newsprint weaving around it symbolizing global communication, and a strong bridge arching over a calm sea beneath, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and gentle blues.

articles.title: As housing insecurity grows, global leaders push for action
articles.summary: “Housing is the gateway to all other rights” said actor and humanitarian Richard Gere, who has joined UN efforts to combat homelessness.
articles.content:
With nearly three billion people lacking access to adequate housing worldwide, the global housing crisis has become one of the most urgent human rights challenges, according to UN-Habitat, the UN agency focused on sustainable urban development and housing.

Tweet URL

More than one billion people live in informal settlements, while over 300 million people experience homelessness across the Global South and North.

In Africa, 62 per cent of urban dwellings are informal. In the Asia-Pacific region, over 500 million people lack access to basic water services, and more than a billion live without adequate sanitation.

In a video message supporting a forthcoming conference, Mr. Gere stressed the urgency of global action. “Without a safe home, there is no health, no education, no stable employment” he said.

Homelessness extends far beyond the absence of shelter. It often results in the denial of fundamental rights, including security, healthcare, privacy and human dignity.

Upcoming forum

The issue will take center stage at the thirteenth session of the World Urban Forum (WUF13), the UN’s premier global conference on sustainable urbanization, due to take place in Baku, Azerbaijan.

Held under the theme “Housing the world: Safe and resilient cities and communities,” the conference will bring together governments, civil society organizations, urban planners, private sector leaders and youth representatives to discuss solutions to the growing housing crisis.

The Gere Foundation, HOGAR SÍ, a Spanish organization focusing on housing issues and UN-Habitat are getting together to focus on how to ensure that all people have access to adequate housing.

Homelessness is rooted in the failure of society to provide for its citizens and addressing the crisis will require long-term political commitment, stronger social protection systems and housing-centered public policies, advocates say.

“Homelessness is not inevitable. It is a profound injustice – one we can solve when we choose dignity, housing and human connection,” Mr. Gere said.

Anacláudia Rossbach, Executive Director of UN-Habitat, said the collaboration highlights the need for global cooperation in confronting urban challenges.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant global human rights issue—housing insecurity and homelessness—and reports on coordinated international efforts, including a major UN conference and collaboration among organizations, to address it. The story focuses on a broad, urgent challenge affecting billions, with concrete actions and commitments aimed at systemic change and improved living conditions worldwide.
articles.personality_title: Global leaders unite to tackle the growing housing crisis
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Nearly three billion people worldwide do not have access to decent housing. This includes over one billion people living in informal settlements and more than 300 million who are homeless. The lack of proper housing affects many regions, such as Africa and Asia-Pacific, where many people also lack clean water and sanitation.

**What happened** – Actor and humanitarian Richard Gere joined the United Nations (UN) in calling for urgent action on homelessness and housing insecurity. He spoke ahead of the World Urban Forum (WUF13), a major UN conference set to take place in Baku, Azerbaijan. This event will bring together governments, experts, and organizations to discuss ways to provide safe and resilient housing for everyone.

**Impact** – The housing crisis is not just about having a roof; it affects health, education, work, and human dignity. This global effort is important because it recognizes housing as a basic human right and aims to create long-lasting solutions. By focusing on stronger social support and better policies, the conference hopes to help millions of people improve their living conditions.

**What's next step** – At the upcoming World Urban Forum, participants will share ideas and make plans to improve housing worldwide. Groups like the Gere Foundation, HOGAR SÍ, and UN-Habitat will work together to push for change. The hope is that this cooperation will lead to real actions by governments and communities to reduce homelessness and build safer cities.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Global leaders and organizations are coming together at a UN conference to find lasting solutions to the housing crisis affecting billions around the world.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les dirigeants mondiaux s'unissent pour lutter contre la crise du logement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Près de trois milliards de personnes dans le monde n'ont pas accès à un logement décent. Parmi elles, plus d'un milliard vivent dans des quartiers informels et plus de 300 millions sont sans abri. Ce manque de logement touche plusieurs régions, comme l'Afrique et l'Asie-Pacifique, où beaucoup n'ont pas non plus accès à l'eau potable et à l'assainissement.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – L'acteur et humanitaire Richard Gere a rejoint les Nations Unies (ONU) pour appeler à une action urgente contre le sans-abrisme et l'insécurité du logement. Il s'est exprimé avant le Forum mondial sur l'urbanisation (WUF13), une grande conférence de l'ONU prévue à Bakou, en Azerbaïdjan. Cet événement réunira gouvernements, experts et organisations pour discuter de solutions afin d'offrir un logement sûr et durable à tous.

**Impact** – La crise du logement ne concerne pas seulement un toit, elle affecte la santé, l'éducation, le travail et la dignité humaine. Cet effort mondial est important car il reconnaît le logement comme un droit humain fondamental et vise des solutions durables. En renforçant les soutiens sociaux et les politiques, la conférence espère améliorer les conditions de vie de millions de personnes.

**Prochaine étape** – Lors du prochain Forum mondial sur l'urbanisation, les participants partageront des idées et établiront des plans pour améliorer le logement dans le monde. Des groupes comme la Fondation Gere, HOGAR SÍ et ONU-Habitat travailleront ensemble pour faire avancer les choses. L'objectif est que cette coopération mène à des actions concrètes des gouvernements et des communautés pour réduire le sans-abrisme et construire des villes plus sûres.

**En une phrase** – Dirigeants et organisations mondiaux se réunissent lors d'une conférence de l'ONU pour trouver des solutions durables à la crise du logement qui touche des milliards de personnes.
articles.personality_title_es: Líderes mundiales se unen para enfrentar la creciente crisis de vivienda
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Casi tres mil millones de personas en el mundo no tienen acceso a una vivienda adecuada. Más de mil millones viven en asentamientos informales y más de 300 millones están sin hogar. Esta falta de vivienda afecta a regiones como África y Asia-Pacífico, donde muchas personas tampoco tienen agua potable ni saneamiento.

**Qué pasó** – El actor y humanitario Richard Gere se unió a las Naciones Unidas (ONU) para pedir una acción urgente contra la falta de vivienda y la inseguridad habitacional. Habló antes del Foro Mundial Urbano (WUF13), una gran conferencia de la ONU que se realizará en Bakú, Azerbaiyán. Este evento reunirá a gobiernos, expertos y organizaciones para discutir cómo ofrecer viviendas seguras y resistentes para todos.

**Impacto** – La crisis de la vivienda no es solo tener un techo; afecta la salud, la educación, el empleo y la dignidad humana. Este esfuerzo global es importante porque reconoce la vivienda como un derecho humano básico y busca soluciones duraderas. Al fortalecer el apoyo social y las políticas, la conferencia espera mejorar las condiciones de vida de millones.

**Próximo paso** – En el próximo Foro Mundial Urbano, los participantes compartirán ideas y harán planes para mejorar la vivienda en todo el mundo. Organizaciones como la Fundación Gere, HOGAR SÍ y ONU-Hábitat trabajarán juntas para impulsar el cambio. Se espera que esta cooperación lleve a acciones reales de gobiernos y comunidades para reducir la falta de vivienda y construir ciudades más seguras.

**Una frase clave** – Líderes y organizaciones globales se reúnen en una conferencia de la ONU para buscar soluciones duraderas a la crisis de vivienda que afecta a miles de millones en el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a diverse cluster of sturdy, colorful miniature houses and shelters gently rising from the hands of multiple silhouetted figures reaching upward together, set against a soft, natural-toned urban landscape at dawn symbolizing hope, unity, and global cooperation to build safe, resilient homes for all.
